# Round-2 rare-word review: AI change suggestions (REPLACE)

*Generated by `scripts/40_split_round2_proposals.py` from the round-2 batch proposal files (restored-text rare words, `rare_words_restored.md`).* Each entry is the king-james agent's suggested replacement with rationale and full NEW verse text.

## abject → outcasts — Psalms 35:15
*(from batch_0001_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: REPLACE
- suggestion: "outcasts" (word_counts KJV_restored = 8; the verse's own CUR text already offers this as an alternate, and UKJV/Geneva reflect the same sense of despised persons)
- NEW: But in mine adversity they rejoiced, and gathered themselves together: yea, the outcasts gathered themselves together against me, and I knew it not; they did tear me, and ceased not:
- rationale: "outcasts" is common in the corpus, matches UKJV's own reading of this verse, and preserves the sense of low/despised persons banding together against the psalmist. Recommend the replacement.

## aboard → went up into it — Acts 21:2
*(from batch_0001_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: REPLACE
- suggestion: "went up into it" in place of "went aboard" (all words independently common; construction drawn from Wycliffe "wenten vp in to it")
- NEW: And finding a ship sailing over unto Phenicia, we went up into it, and set forth.
- rationale: "aboard" is a single rare hapax; the phrase "went up into it" carries the identical sense of embarking using only common corpus words and keeps the verb tense unchanged. Recommend the replacement.

## besiegers → spoilers — Jeremiah 4:16
*(from batch_0002_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: REPLACE
- suggestion: "spoilers" (count 8) — a common KJV word for raiders/plunderers that compass a city, matching the sense of armed men come to besiege.
- NEW: Make ye mention to the nations; behold, publish against Jerusalem, that spoilers come from a far country, and give out their voice against the cities of Judah.
- rationale: "besiegers" is a corpus hapax; "spoilers" is well attested (8) and names the same referent (hostile raiders approaching a city) without inventing new vocabulary. Witnesses split between "watchers" and "besiegers/attackers" — "spoilers" sits comfortably in that semantic field.

Summary of verdicts: 44 REPLACE, 2 NO-SAFE-SWAP (cricket, deformed), 0 WHITELIST, across 48 verse entries in 40 groups.

## everyone → every one — Matthew 7:26
*(from batch_0004_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: REPLACE
- suggestion: split into the two-word KJV-style form "every one" (both words common: "every" 1235, "one" 1976 occurrences).
- NEW: And every one that heareth these sayings of Mine, and doeth them not, shall be likened unto a foolish man, who built his house upon the sand:
- rationale: "everyone" as a single closed compound is a modern spelling; the period convention (and every witness above) is the two-word "every one." No self-certifying appeal to KJV needed — the words themselves are ordinary and abundant in the corpus.

## everywhere → every where — Mark 16:20
*(from batch_0004_proposals_v2.md)*
- verdict: REPLACE
- suggestion: split into "every where" (two common words: "every" 1235, "where" 400).
- NEW: And they went forth, and preached every where, the Lord working with them, and confirming the Word with signs following. Amen.
- rationale: Same modern-compounding issue as "everyone." "Every where" is the ordinary period spacing; both components are frequent, unremarkable words.

## exactor → collector — Daniel 11:20
*(from batch_0004_proposals_v2.md)*

- suggestion: "collector" (attested 6×) in place of the rare technical noun "exactor," preserving the sentence's structure.

- rationale: "Exactor" names a tax-official referent; "collector" carries the same office in plain, well-attested English without inventing new content. Minimal structural change keeps the verse's cadence intact.
